text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Sharma  
Riverbank Medical Centre  
48 Mill Lane, London NW6 1NJ  

Dear Dr Priya Sharma,  

Alexander Morton, DOB: 22-07-1991, Hospital No: M847520 NHS No. 1049382756  
Mooring 17, Regent’s Canal, London N1 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with patient-reported seizures occurring predominantly when markedly underslept/past concussive head injury in adolescence without persistent deficit/seasonal allergic rhinitis/vitamin D insufficiency/lives on a houseboat with intermittent electricity supply impacting device charging/smoker (5/day). 

Medication: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily.  
Clonazepam 0.5 mg at night as rescue for clusters (patient holds supply).  
Cetirizine 10 mg once daily as needed for rhinitis.  
Cholecalciferol 1,000 IU daily.  

I reviewed Alex in clinic today. He attended alone. He lives on a narrowboat and reports that occasional power cuts disrupt his ability to keep his phone and seizure-alert smartwatch charged. He keeps a paper log. He feels generally well, works part-time doing boat maintenance, and reports regular outdoor activity. Weight stable; sleep pattern variable due to early lock navigation times and intermittent generator noise. No recent intercurrent illness. 

Seizures: History is patient-reported. He describes brief generalised tonic–clonic seizures without clear focal onset. He reports that events arise almost exclusively after nights of curtailed sleep, such as when moored near noisy locks or after early starts for travel; on well-rested weeks he remains seizure-free. Over the past six months he recorded: April x 0, May x 1 GTC following two consecutive short nights, June x 0, July x 0, August x 1 GTC after a night with less than three hours’ sleep during a power outage, September x 0. No myoclonic jerks reported on non-sleep-deprived days. Post-ictal confusion lasts ~20–30 minutes with tongue bite on two occasions, no injuries, no incontinence. No known focal aware or impaired-awareness seizures. There is a family history of “fits” in a maternal uncle. He does not drive. 

Examination: Alert and oriented, normal speech and language. Cranial nerves intact. Motor and coordination normal. No gait abnormality. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. 

Investigations: Prior EEG (2023) reportedly showed generalised spike–wave discharges with photoparoxysmal response; MRI brain (2023) normal. Recent LFTs (August 2025) within reference range; valproate level not recently checked. Vitamin D borderline low (55 nmol/L) in April 2025. 

Assessment: Patient with generalised epilepsy with seizures tending to occur when significantly sleep-restricted. Overall control is acceptable with infrequent events linked to erratic sleep opportunities on the houseboat. Rescue clonazepam rarely used. Risk heightened by inconsistent charging of alert devices during power outages. 

Plan: 
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily; no dose change today. 
- Check trough valproate level and repeat LFTs and FBC in the next 2–3 weeks at practice. 
- Reinforce regular sleep routine where feasible; patient will trial a portable power bank to maintain phone/watch charge during outages (patient-initiated). 
- Safety: maintain seizure diary; avoid swimming alone; ensure mooring neighbours have ICE contact details. 
- Continue vitamin D supplementation; recheck level with next bloods. 
- No driving; he understands current DVLA requirements. 
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if seizure frequency increases or if events begin to occur despite adequate sleep. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past six months he recorded: April x 0, May x 1 GTC following two consecutive short nights, June x 0, July x 0, August x 1 GTC after a night with less than three hours’ sleep during a power outage, September x 0.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly lists seizure counts over the past six months: April 0, May 1, June 0, July 0, August 1, September 0. Total = 2 seizures in 6 months. Convert to an allowed frequency format: 2 per 6 months simplifies to 1 per 3 months. The patient is not seizure-free for ≥6 months because the last seizure was in August.
seizure_frequency_number: Over the past six months he recorded: April x 0, May x 1 GTC following two consecutive short nights, June x 0, July x 0, August x 1 GTC after a night with less than three hours’ sleep during a power outage, September x 0.

flaws.description: Gold (1 per 3 month) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
